mosquitto_new_vulns.description: CONNECT with all fields maximally populated
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker gave no CONNACK to oversized CONNECT — possible DoS or parsing failure

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: SUBSCRIBE with 500 topic filters in one packet
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker granted all 500 subscriptions in one packet. Failures: 0/500. No per-client subscription limit enforced.

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: 1000 QoS0 PUBLISH at maximum rate — rate limit check
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker accepted 1000 PUBLISH at 104931 msg/sec without rate limiting. No per-connection publish quota enforced.

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: 200 PINGREQ to hold connection without real traffic
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker responded to 200/200 PINGREQs without any data traffic. Keep-alive abuse allows connection maintenance without meaningful traffic. No PINGREQ rate limiting detected.

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: Subscribe QoS2, publisher sends QoS0 — silent downgrade check
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Subscriber requested QoS 2 but received message at QoS 0. QoS downgrade occurs silently — subscriber has no notification that delivery guarantees are reduced. This can be a reliability/trust violation in systems that assume QoS 2 delivery semantics.

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: MQTT v5 DISCONNECT (with reason code) sent to v3.1.1 broker
mosquitto_new_vulns.notes: Broker silently accepted v5 DISCONNECT and remains alive (best case)

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: Subscribe to a/b, a/+, # simultaneously — message multiplication
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker delivered 263 copies of 1 message due to overlapping subscriptions (a/b, a/+, #). While spec-compliant (§3.3.5), this enables bandwidth amplification: 1 PUBLISH → 263x deliveries to a single subscriber. A single attacker client can amplify their own traffic by {copies}x.
mosquitto_new_vulns.notes: Received 263 message copies for 1 published message

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: PUBACK for unrequested packet ID — state machine confusion
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker silently accepts PUBACK for packet IDs it never sent. While not immediately exploitable, this indicates absence of strict QoS state validation — supports the V10 finding with additional coverage.
mosquitto_new_vulns.notes: Broker silently tolerated 6 orphan PUBACKs (expected benign behavior)

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: PUBLISH/SUBSCRIBE with invalid UTF-8 topic names
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail:
Broker silently accepts PUBLISH with invalid UTF-8 byte sequences in topic names. Per MQTT §1.5.3, brokers SHOULD reject packets with invalid UTF-8. Accepting invalid UTF-8 enables topic namespace injection and may cause unexpected behavior in subscribers that validate topic encoding.
Sequences tested: fffe, 8080, c0af, eda080, f880808080, 76616c69642f, 00746f706963, 746f70696300

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: Keep-alive=2s, wait 5s — broker must disconnect per §3.1.2.10
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Broker did not disconnect client after 5.5s of inactivity with keepalive=2s (should have disconnected within 3s per §3.1.2.10). Zombie connections can accumulate, enabling slow connection exhaustion attacks.

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: Will message targets own subscribed topic — self-delivery check
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Client's Will message (QoS 0, non-retain) was delivered to the observer when client disconnected ungracefully. When the Will topic equals a topic the dead client was subscribed to, a persistent session would receive its own Will on reconnect. Combined with V1 (Will injection) and V3 (session hijacking), this enables cross-client information injection via Will messages.

Broker survived 50 rapid SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE cycles. Got 4 intermediate responses. Post-race message delivery: YES. No crash detected but rapid alternation without rate limiting is a denial-of-service surface — subscription table churn at high rate.

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: Retained message redelivered on re-subscribe after unsubscribe
mosquitto_new_vulns.notes: First delivery: True, Second delivery: None

mosquitto_new_vulns.description: QoS2 flow interrupted by ClientID session takeover
mosquitto_new_vulns.anomaly_detail: Hijacker successfully took over a session with an in-flight QoS 2 transaction. session_present=1 confirms existing session transferred. Post-hijack messages received: 0. The broker transferred unresolved QoS 2 PUBREL/PUBCOMP state to the hijacker, enabling the attacker to complete or abandon QoS 2 transactions initiated by the victim.

mitigation_verification.detail: Retained message still delivered — ACL did not block PUBLISH
Second CONNECT RC=0x00, session_present=1 (HIJACK SUCCEEDED — mitigation failed)
$SYS data still delivered — ACL did not block $SYS subscription
Anon CONNECT RC=0x00 (ACCEPTED — auth not enforced)
No DISCONNECT sent for QoS1 PID=0 — broker still accepts invalid PID
100 connections: 100 succeeded in 0.14s (732/s) (NO RATE LIMIT — mitigation failed)
Version string still delivered to anonymous client — $SYS not restricted

results.anomaly_detail: Will message delivered to admin/control without authorization
Hijacker got CONNACK RC=0, session_present=1. Victim's session was taken over.
3/4 anomalous credential patterns accepted

results.notes: No response to QoS1 PID=0 — likely connection dropped (correct)
